subject: Cleanup all Param objects when any rules change
commit_message:
Cleanup all Param objects when any rules change

Fix license date for new junit test.
Updated some comments.
Ensured different control loop names in different rule sets of new junit
test.
Used different mechanism for examining facts in new junit test.
Fixed typo in comment.
Changed the way new junit checks for existence of facts.
Fixed another typo in comment.
Updated clean-up rules in CLC template and added junit test to verify
that Params are cleaned up appropriately.
Removed code that retracts $manager objects so that the manager clean-up
rule will be used, instead, thus ensuring that all associated objects are
deleted when the manager is deleted.  This was done for amsterdam, CLC,
and casablanca templates.
Added an extra fireAllRules() after calls to fireUntilHalt() to enable
clean-up rules to fire in junit tests.
Address Josh's review comments.
Enhance junit test to compare actual object references to ensure that
Param objects are as expected.

Change-Id: If1fefd3f57e417180c62a3849defbaa6ae83afc1
Issue-ID: POLICY-1248
Signed-off-by: Jim Hahn <jrh3@att.com>

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

(3 comments)

I think there may be a bug in the logic here (or I am just misunderstanding how things work). The tests as written should still complete correctly, but if I'm correct, there will be lots of paramCleaner instances that build up in memory (which the current tests don't look for)

Patch Set 1:

(3 comments)

Patch Set 1:

> (3 comments)
 > 
 > I think there may be a bug in the logic here (or I am just
 > misunderstanding how things work). The tests as written should
 > still complete correctly, but if I'm correct, there will be lots of
 > paramCleaner instances that build up in memory (which the current
 > tests don't look for)

PARAMS.CLEANED is responsible for removing ParamCleaners when the clean-up has completed.  FYI: the junit test verifies that there are only two objects in memory, one for each control loop.

Patch Set 2:

Thank you Jim and Josh for looking in detail into this code review.

Given at this stage of the release where we are, it's critical to make sure that there is no breakage associated with these changes in the runtime operation of the use cases.   This is unless there is an extension on the release dates (Pam can weigh in).  

If we are not confident about not incurring in additional breakage, and there is no dates extension, it would be better to complete the release with an exception mentioning a "restart" workaround for the upgrade case that Jim is trying to handle.

Feedback?

Patch Set 2: -Code-Review

What I can say is that Jim's code looks good to me based on the review that I've done and the exchange with Jim (in fact, having caught only two minor bugs gives me a bit extra faith in the code). That said, Jim will need to weigh in on his level of confidence on this not incurring in additional breakage.

Patch Set 2:

sorry, misspoke one minor bug and some minor cleanup.
Patch Set 2: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 2:

> Thank you Jim and Josh for looking in detail into this code review.
 > 
 > Given at this stage of the release where we are, it's critical to
 > make sure that there is no breakage associated with these changes
 > in the runtime operation of the use cases.   This is unless there
 > is an extension on the release dates (Pam can weigh in).
 > 
 > If we are not confident about not incurring in additional breakage,
 > and there is no dates extension, it would be better to complete the
 > release with an exception mentioning a "restart" workaround for the
 > upgrade case that Jim is trying to handle.
 > 
 > Feedback?

Jorge, I'm never 100% sure about anything, though I'm reasonably confident this will solve the problem without breaking anything.  The problem is, if I'm wrong and it does break something, there isn't much time to fix it.  In addition, I did realize after already starting to work this issue:
1) this problem has been around since Beijing.  Given that, waiting another release cycle doesn't seem critical
2) if you're truly comfortable saying that a restart is required every time a policy is deleted OR updated, then that sounds like a suitable workaround for now
So I'm OK either way.

Patch Set 2: Code-Review+1

Please hold off on submitting this right now.

Patch Set 2: Code-Review-2

I am really uncomfortable with having to restart the Drools controller manually everytime a rule is deleted/updated. But Jorge has a point. Please let me work with Integration team to determine if we can push this in now and get it tested.

I will undo the -2 when we agree, please keep the review open.

Patch Set 2: Code-Review+1

Based on that the update yaml case, which is a high runner, seems to be working OK (from drools perspective), I think it is an improvement, and I am OK with merging it.

I have seen that issue off and on while creating the junit test.  As near as I can tell, it's a bug in drools, itself.  I'm not sure what happens in this case, whether the engine becomes completely corrupted or not, so that may inform your decision as to whether or not to merge this.

Patch Set 2:

The engine does not get corrupted for what I see, able to process messages.
The only issue is that the in the delete control loop.  Those control loops Param facts don't get retracted.
In any case, I think it is an improvement.   The delete control loop in theory is a low runner scenario, in comparison with the update.

Patch Set 2:

Ok, we can fix any remaining delete issues in a separate commit.
Patch Set 2: -Code-Review
Patch Set 2: Code-Review+2
Change has been successfully merged by Pamela Dragosh
